transcript: [patient] amanda taylor , birth date october 31 , 1949 . patient is a 72-year-old woman who comes for follow-up for hypertension . her history includes significant alcohol use disorder and my mild hypercholesterolemia . at last visit on april 16 , her blood pressure was 130/90 . after presenting at 150/100 , she was prescribed hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg by mouth once daily and lisinopril 20 mg by mouth once daily . cmp and cbc were essentially unremarkable . [doctor] hi miss taylor , how are you today ? [patient] hi , i'm , i'm good , thank you . [doctor] thanks for coming in today . [patient] yeah , no problem . [doctor] so how have things been going for you ? [patient] they have been alright . i'm struggling with not drinking . i wanted to discuss that shot that you had mentioned last time but we did n't get to discuss it too much . [doctor] okay well i will be happy to provide you with more information . so tell me when did you last have a drink ? [patient] on monday . [doctor] okay , so you've gone a couple of days without it ? [patient] yeah , yeah , i have . [doctor] yeah , and had you been drinking for a while ? [patient] yep . [doctor] how much had , how much had you been drinking ? [patient] four or five times a week , hard alcohol so you know . [doctor] okay . well , it will certainly help your blood pressure if you are able to stop drinking . [patient] yeah , yeah , i , i , i agree with you . [doctor] okay . are you taking your blood pressure medications ? [patient] yeah , but i'm only taking half of the lisinopril . [doctor] okay . [patient] the well the first time i took it , i felt lethargic and tired so i only took a half dose the next time . i felt much better and i did n't have the headaches or the swishing that started in my left ear so i feel like it's definitely helping but i do n't know but i know it's not gon na be okay until i stop drinking . [doctor] and are you also taking the full dose of the hydrochlorothiazide ? do you have any chest tightness or pressure or any shortness of breath ? [patient] yeah , i'm taking the full dose of that medicine too and no , none of those symptoms . [doctor] okay . anything else i should be aware of or that we should be checking ? [patient] hmmm no . [doctor] okay . good . are you doing the blood pressure checks at home ? [patient] yes , yes , i am . [doctor] okay , good . what kind of numbers are you seeing ? [patient] well , in the morning i'm seeing lower numbers than in the evening where i run anywhere from like 130-145 or 150/95-100 on the bottom . [doctor] okay . [patient] but in the evenings if i sit there for a while and then take it , it goes down . kind of like when i come here , it's always high , but then it goes down . in the mornings , it's generally more normal where it's supposed to be . [doctor] uh uh . [patient] hmmm also i'm taking my medicine in the morning when i get up instead of in the evening . [doctor] yeah so it sounds like we are not too far out of where we want to be . [patient] right . [doctor] mm-hmm . okay . well , let me get a listen to your heart , go ahead and hop up here . [patient] okay . [doctor] use my general exam template . alright , go ahead and take some deep breaths . okay , good . any fever , cough , shortness of breath , or sore throat ? no . any recent travel or contact or exposure to the coronavirus ? [patient] nope . [doctor] have you received your corona virus vaccine ? [patient] yes on february 9 . [doctor] okay great alright well i'm just going to look at your ankles and feet , i'm looking for any signs of swelling . [patient] okay . [doctor] alright , well everything looks good . you can go ahead and sit up miss taylor . alright , so the good news is everything is looking okay . i think we both agree if you were able to stop drinking . your blood pressure would cease to be an issue for you . yeah , i agree with that . they will let us discuss the potential medicine to help you with that . what it does is it blocks some of the brain receptors that alcohol stimulates and it tricks the brain into thinking it does not need alcohol thereby cutting down the urges . [patient] the urge to drink or they constant thinking about it ? [doctor] it actually will help with both . [patient] okay , so is it like a one time thing ? [doctor] generally the shot is given about once a month , it can also be given as a daily pill but the shot does tend to work a bit better . [patient] yeah , i have taken things like this in the past and if it is a pill , i know i wo n't take it . with the shot , it's not like you can undoke it . [doctor] yeah , that's true and the shot has been shown to work , it is more just a matter of getting you in to get your shot a month later with the nurse . [patient] yeah , sure , sure . [doctor] okay , so it's called naltrexone . i think the other name might be vivitrol . like i said , the research on it is pretty good . [patient] i think i may have taken the naltrexone before . [doctor] okay , yeah , maybe you have . [patient] yeah , if it was available in a pill form , then i'm almost certain i tried it before . [doctor] okay . if this sounds good though , we can go ahead and get you started with the shot . i am looking and it looks like we have you coming back in on may 17 for your first shot . does that sound good ? [patient] yeah , yeah , that sounds great . are there any possible side effects ? [doctor] yeah , some people will experience some nausea , headache , dizziness , anxiety , anxiety , tiredness and trouble sleeping but generally the side effects are mild and should go away within a few days . [patient] okay . [doctor] then we can schedule you for a follow-up in june to see how you're doing . [patient] alright , that sounds good . [doctor] alright , great , well otherwise , stay with your current medicines , keep checking your blood pressure , and let's see if we can stop the alcohol for good . let us know if you have any questions or any issues come up after you received the first dose . [patient] alright , sounds good . thank you . [doctor] you're welcome . have a good rest of your day . [patient] you too . [doctor] miss taylor is drinking alcohol 4 to 5 days/week to excess . no tobacco , no substance abuse . skin is moist . good trigger . on exam , she is alert , pleasant , in no acute distress . she is not diabetic . there is no tremor . no cva tenderness . chest clear to percussion in a suscultation . unlabored breathing . cardiac rhythm regular . no murmur , no gallop . jv flat with a head at 90 degrees . she does not appear anxious , agitated , or depressed . my impression , alcohol use disorder . she would like to start naltrexone which we have previously discussed . she will take the injectable form and start on may 17 . we reviewed side effects and risks , the potential benefit of alcohol cessation would be highly beneficial to her for many reasons . hypertension , she is taking lisinopril 10 mg daily and hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg daily . quite possibly her blood pressure will improve completely with cessation of alcohol . her target bp is 130-140/70-80 . i will see her back week of june 14 which is likely around the time of her second naltrexone injection . i recommend upgrading influenza vaccination and shingrix . # 2 , she did defer this .
gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Hypertension.
Alcohol use disorder.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Amanda Taylor is an 72-year-old woman who presents for a follow-up for hypertension. History includes significant alcohol use disorder and mild hypercholesterolemia. At last visit on April 16, BP was 130/90. After presenting at 150/100 she was prescribed hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg by mouth once daily and lisinopril 20 mg by mouth once daily. CMP and CBC were essentially unremarkable.

Mrs. Taylor admits to consuming alcohol 4-5 times a week and is interested in Naltrexone injections. She acknowledges that her blood pressure would likely improve if she stopped alcohol consumption. She is checking her blood pressure in the morning and evening, stating it is higher at night but will improve after sitting for a few minutes. She is averaged 135-145 or 150 over 95-110.

She experienced side effects from lisinopril, such as headaches, "swooshing" in her left ear, and lethargy. She took half a dose and did not experience the side effects.

PAST HISTORY

Medical
Hypertension.
Hypercholesterolemia.
Alcohol use disorder.

CURRENT MEDICATIONS

Lisinopril 20 mg tablet once daily.
Hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg once daily.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Constitutional
No distress. No fever. No tobacco or other substance use.

Neurological
Alert.

Psychological
She does not appear anxious, agitated or depressed.

Neck
JVP flat with a head at 90 degrees.

Respiratory
No cough or shortness of breath. Chest clear to percussion and auscultation. Unlabored breathing.

Cardiovascular
No CVA tenderness. Cardiac rhythm regular. No murmur. No gallop.

Musculoskeletal
No ankle edema.

Integumentary
Skin is moist. Good trigger.

ASSESSMENT

 Alcohol use disorder.
 Hypertension.

Mrs. Taylor presents for a follow-up for hypertension. History includes significant alcohol use disorder and mild hypercholesterolemia. At last visit on April 16, BP was 130/90. After presenting at 150/100 she was prescribed hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg by mouth once daily and lisinopril 20 mg by mouth once daily. CMP and CBC were essentially unremarkable.

PLAN

Alcohol use disorder
I will start her on naltrexone which we have previously discussed. She will take the injectable form and start on May 17. We reviewed side effects and risks. The potential benefit of alcohol cessation would be highly beneficial to the patient.

Hypertension
She will continue taking lisinopril 10 mg/day and hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg daily. Quite possibly her blood pressure will improve significantly if she is able to completely stop consuming alcohol. Target BP is 130-140/70-80. I will see her back week of June 14 which likely will be the time of her second naltrexone injection.

Recommend upgrading influenza vaccination and Shingrix #2, she defers this.

INSTRUCTIONS

Call the clinic with any questions. Make follow up appointment week of June 14th.
notes:
Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Amanda Taylor, a 72-year-old woman, presents for follow-up for hypertension. She has a history of significant alcohol use disorder and mild hypercholesterolemia. Her last visit was on April 16, with a blood pressure of 130/90. She has been struggling with not drinking and has had a history of drinking 4 to 5 days a week, consuming hard alcohol. She has tried to stop drinking, but has been experiencing withdrawal symptoms. She is currently taking lisinopril 10 mg daily and hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg daily.

Past medical history: The patient has a history of alcohol use disorder and mild hypercholesterolemia. She has received the coronavirus vaccine on February 9.

Review of systems: The patient denies fever, cough, shortness of breath, or sore throat. She has not had recent travel or contact with the coronavirus.

Current medications:
- Lisinopril 10 mg daily
- Hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg daily

Objective:
Vital signs: Not mentioned
Physical exam: The patient is alert, pleasant, and in no acute distress. Her skin is moist. She is not diabetic. There is no tremor. No CVA tenderness. Her chest is clear to percussion and auscultation. Her breathing is unlabored. Her cardiac rhythm is regular. No murmur or gallop. Her JVP is flat with a head at 90 degrees. She does not appear anxious, agitated, or depressed.

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has alcohol use disorder, which is confirmed by her history of drinking 4 to 5 days a week and her struggles with withdrawal symptoms. The patient would like to start naltrexone to help with alcohol cessation.
Problem list: Alcohol use disorder; Hypertension; Hypercholesterolemia; None other.

Plan:
I prescribed naltrexone, which will be administered via injection on May 17. We reviewed the potential benefits and side effects of the medication, including nausea, headache, dizziness, anxiety, tiredness, and trouble sleeping. I also recommended upgrading her influenza vaccination and Shingrix, which she deferred. I will see her back on June 14 for a follow-up, which is likely around the time of her second naltrexone injection.

Follow-up: The patient will return on June 14 for a follow-up appointment, which is likely around the time of her second naltrexone injection.

At her last visit on April 16, her blood pressure was 130/90. She has been prescribed hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg by mouth once daily and lisinopril 20 mg by mouth once daily. She has been experiencing some difficulty with drinking and wants to discuss the shot that was mentioned at her previous visit. She reports drinking 4-5 times a week, with hard alcohol. She has been able to stop drinking for a couple of days, but feels that it is not okay until she stops drinking entirely. She has been taking her medications, but only taking half the dose of lisinopril due to side effects. She reports that taking the full dose of hydrochlorothiazide. She denies chest tightness or pressure, shortness of breath, and other symptoms. She is doing blood pressure checks at home and reports seeing numbers ranging from 130-145/95-100, with lower numbers in the morning. She is taking her medication in the morning instead of the evening. She reports no fever, cough, shortness of breath, or sore throat, and has not received any recent travel or contact with the coronavirus. She has received the coronavirus vaccine on February 9. She has no allergies.

Past medical history: Significant alcohol use disorder, mild hypercholesterolemia, hypertension.

- Hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg by mouth once daily

Cardiac rhythm is regular. No murmur, no gallop. Jv is flat with a head at 90 degrees. She does not appear anxious, agitated, or depressed.

- CMP and CBC were essentially unremarkable.

Assessment: The patient has hypertension and significant alcohol use disorder.

Plan:

I will see her back in the week of June 14, which is likely around the time of her second naltrexone injection. I recommend upgrading her influenza vaccination and Shingrix #2, which she deferred.
